focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

Doug drove to Dayton on a Saturday because Saturdays were when Gloria expected him and because Gloria did not accommodate deviations from her expectations. The drive was seventy miles on I-70, flat and straight, the fields on either side still brown but showing the first green suggestion of spring, as though Ohio were considering it but hadn't committed. Doug's Civic needed an oil change. The indicator light had been on for eleven days and he'd been meaning to deal with it but hadn't, and the orange glow on the dashboard was the kind of small failure he understood — quantifiable, fixable, ignorable for a while longer.

He was not thinking about Indianapolis. He was specifically and deliberately not thinking about the Holiday Inn bedspread or the nineteen minutes that should have been twenty-five or the polite applause that was worse than silence. He was thinking about oil changes and exit numbers and whether he should have brought Gloria flowers, which he hadn't, because the last time he'd brought Gloria flowers she had said they were the wrong kind and set them on the counter and never mentioned them again.

Maple Glen Senior Living looked like what it was — a one-story complex off Route 35 with a parking lot that was always half-empty and landscaping that someone maintained without enthusiasm. The sign out front had a maple leaf in the logo. There were no maple trees on the property. Doug had pointed this out to Gloria once. She had said, "There's a motel on Route 40 called the Paradise. There is no paradise on Route 40."

The woman at the front desk was named Angela. She wore a lanyard with a photo ID that showed a version of herself smiling more broadly than the one behind the desk. She gave him the standard "Here to see your mom?" and Doug said, "Yes,...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...uses where people ate lamb on a Tuesday. He turned onto the highway and then into Clintonville, where the houses were closer together and the porches had the particular cluttered look of people who used them. He parked in the lot behind his building, where the pavement was cracked and the lines were faded and the space was his because he always parked in the same one.

He carried the leftovers inside. He set them in the refrigerator, which contained a half-gallon of milk approaching its date, a block of cheddar with one corner going dark, and a jar of mustard with maybe one more serving before it was empty. The warm foil package sat on the middle shelf between the cheddar and the mustard, giving off a little heat in the cold air.

He stood in the kitchen. His kitchen. The light over the stove was on -- he left it burning at night because the apartment felt less empty with one light going, which was not something he had ever said to anyone or planned to. The kitchen was small. The counter was laminate, beige, a color that had been chosen by someone who didn't care what color the counter was and would never be asked. There was no island, no stone, no copper pots. There was a toaster with a broken lever that he had to hold down manually, and a coffee maker that took seven minutes for four cups, and a cereal bowl drying in the rack that was the same cereal bowl he used every night.

He set the foil package on the counter beside the cereal bowl and left it there for a minute. The heat from it spread into the laminate. Under the stove light the foil looked almost white.

He unwrapped the foil. The crackle of it filled the kitchen for a second. The lamb was still warm. He ate it standing at the counter, in his kitchen, under the stove light, with his hands.

It was still good.
